title: Lab06: Logistic Regression
text:
Task 1: Direction of influence [1 point]
Specify with common sense arguments into which directions all independent variables may influence the wife’s propensity to participate in the labor force. Use a table to with the headings [a] variable name, [b] argument and [c] null and alternative hypotheses for your answer.

title: Lab06: Logistic Regression
text:
Task 2: Model discussion [3 points]
[a] Build a logistic regression model for the probability of lfp with these independent variables and give the 95% confidence intervals around the estimated logistic regression parameters.

title: Lab06: Logistic Regression
section: Task 3: Perform a likelihood ratio test [0.5 point]
text:
Task 3: Perform a likelihood ratio test [0.5 point]
Refine the model from task 2 by dropping all variables which you deem to be not relevant. Test whether these variables jointly have explanatory power or not. Properly state the null and the alternative hypotheses.

[a] The measurement units of PCTPOPPOV do not match the expected range of probabilities \(0≤\Pr(PCTPOPPOV_i)≤1\). Look the definition of this variable up in the online help. Perform a proper scaling of the variable PCTPOPPOV. Justify your selected transformation.

title: Lab04: Heteroscedasticity
text:

Demonstrate your iteratively re-weighted function wlm with the Journals dataframe from Kleiber & Zeilig’s package AER. Make sure to use the log-transformed dependent and independent variables. Show the results of the weighted lm-model with summary(lm(y~x, weights= 1/w^gamma2)). (1 point)

title: Lab04: Heteroscedasticity
text:
Task 2: Evaluate Model Y1~x (1 point)
Estimate the plain model lm(Y1~x) and compare it to the model lmHetero(...) with properly specified weights variable w. What pattern of residual variances does the \(\gamma_1\) indicate and do we observed significant heteroscedasticity? Do the estimated parameters of the weighted model change substantially? Do the regression coefficients standard errors change substantially?

Task 1: Partial Regression Coefficient [2 points]
Use the CONCORD1.SAV file for this task. You will demonstrate that in multiple regression the partial effect of an independent variable is free from any linear effects of the remaining independent variables in a regression model.
1.1 Run the multiple model water81~income+water80+educat and interpret its regression coefficients. [0.5 points]

Your answer goes here

answer 1-1

rm(list=ls())                          # Clear environment
oldpar <- par()                        # save default graphical parameters
cat("\014")                            # Clear the Console

library(car)

Warning: package 'car' was built under R version 4.5.3


Loading required package: carData

concord <- foreign::read.spss("Concord1.sav", to.data.frame=TRUE)

## Multiple Regression Model
mod1.lm <- lm(water81 ~ income + water80 + educat, data=concord)
summary(mod1.lm)


Call:
lm(formula = water81 ~ income + water80 + educat, data = concord)

Residuals:
    Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
-4635.2  -473.4   -65.0   405.6  4831.1 

Coefficients:
             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept) 811.18243  196.29879   4.132 4.22e-05 ***
income       24.71652    3.54917   6.964 1.06e-11 ***
water80       0.59130    0.02477  23.872  < 2e-16 ***
educat      -49.88881   14.18671  -3.517 0.000478 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 914.9 on 492 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.6233,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.621 
F-statistic: 271.3 on 3 and 492 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16


The multiple regression model water81 ~ income + water80 + educat produces the following results:
The model fits the data reasonably well, with an adjusted R² of 0.621, meaning that income, prior water use, and education together explain about 62% of the variation in 1981 household water consumption. The overall F-test is highly significant (F = 271.3, p < 0.001), so the model as a whole is statistically meaningful.
Intercept (811.18, p < 0.001): When income, water80, and educat are all zero, the predicted 1981 water use would be about 811 gallons. This value doesn’t have much practical meaning since a household with zero income and zero prior water use is unrealistic.
income (24.72, p < 0.001): Holding water80 and educat constant, each additional $1,000 of household income is associated with an increase of about 24.72 gallons in 1981 water consumption. This suggests that wealthier households tend to use more water even after controlling for past usage habits and education level.
water80 (0.591, p < 0.001): Holding income and educat constant, each additional gallon used in 1980 predicts an increase of about 0.59 gallons in 1981 water use. This makes intuitive sense — households tend to maintain their water consumption habits over time, so past usage is a strong baseline predictor of current usage.
educat (−49.89, p < 0.001): Holding income and water80 constant, each additional year of education is associated with a decrease of about 49.89 gallons in 1981 water consumption. This negative relationship might reflect that more educated households are more aware of conservation efforts.

1.2 Calculate the residuals of the two models [a] water81~educat+water80 and [b] income~educat+water80. What are these residuals specifically measuring? [0.5 points]

Your answer goes here


answer 1-2

## Model [a] water81~educat+water80
mod1a.lm <- lm(water81 ~ educat + water80, data=concord)
summary(mod1a.lm)


Call:
lm(formula = water81 ~ educat + water80, data = concord)

Residuals:
    Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
-4849.4  -424.4   -95.3   359.4  5289.0 

Coefficients:
             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept) 766.16393  205.42601   3.730 0.000214 ***
educat      -16.86832   14.00016  -1.205 0.228833    
water80       0.64730    0.02453  26.387  < 2e-16 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 958 on 493 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.5861,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.5845 
F-statistic: 349.1 on 2 and 493 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16

## Model [b] income~educat+water80
mod1b.lm <- lm(income ~ educat + water80, data=concord)
summary(mod1b.lm)


Call:
lm(formula = income ~ educat + water80, data = concord)

Residuals:
    Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
-27.224  -8.241  -1.459   5.839  66.174 

Coefficients:
              Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept) -1.8213936  2.4896108  -0.732    0.465    
educat       1.3359684  0.1696716   7.874 2.21e-14 ***
water80      0.0022653  0.0002973   7.620 1.31e-13 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 11.61 on 493 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.2126,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.2094 
F-statistic: 66.56 on 2 and 493 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16

## Calculate Residuals
e.water81 <- residuals(mod1a.lm)   # Residuals from model [a]: water81|educat+water80
e.income  <- residuals(mod1b.lm)   # Residuals from model [b]: income|educat+water80

Model [a]: water81 ~ educat + water80
The residuals from this model represent the variation in 1981 water consumption that cannot be explained by education level or prior water use (water80). In other words, after statistically removing the linear effects of educat and water80 from water81, what remains is the “pure” variation in 1981 water use that is free from any linear influence of those two variables. This leftover variation is what income will later try to explain.
Model [b]: income ~ educat + water80
Similarly, the residuals from this model represent the variation in household income that cannot be explained by education level or prior water use. After removing the linear effects of educat and water80 from income, what remains is the “pure” variation in income that is independent of those two control variables.
The reason we need both sets of residuals:
The key idea here is that in multiple regression, the partial coefficient of income reflects its relationship with water81 after the shared linear effects of educat and water80 have already been accounted for. By extracting the residuals from both models, we are essentially “cleaning” both variables — we are left with the part of water81 and the part of income that have nothing to do with educat or water80. The relationship between these two cleaned-up residuals is precisely what the partial regression coefficient of income captures in the full multiple regression model.

1.3 Generate the partial regression leverage scatterplot of the water residuals against the income residuals. Make sure to use properly labeled axes. Briefly interpret the scatterplot. [0.5 points]

Your answer goes here


answer 1-3

## Partial Regression Leverage Plot
## water81 residuals ~ income residuals
## (after removing linear effects of educat+water80)
car::scatterplot(e.water81 ~ e.income,
            xlab=bquote(hat(bold(e))["Income|educat+water80"]),
            ylab=bquote(hat(bold(e))["Water81|educat+water80"]),
            smooth=list(span=0.5, lty.smooth=1, col.smooth="red", col.var="red"),
            regLine=list(col="green"))








The partial regression leverage scatterplot shows the relationship between the residuals of water81 (after removing the linear effects of educat and water80) plotted against the residuals of income (after removing the same linear effects of educat and water80). In other words, both axes represent the “leftover” variation in each variable that cannot be explained by education level or prior water use.
The green regression line shows a clear positive slope, indicating that households with higher-than-expected income (given their education and prior water use) also tend to have higher-than-expected 1981 water consumption. This positive relationship reflects the pure partial effect of income on water81, completely free from any linear influence of educat and water80.
However, there are a number of outliers visible in the plot, particularly some observations with very high water residuals and a few extreme income residuals on the right side of the plot. These points may have noticeable influence on the slope estimate. The loess smoother (red curve) follows a similar upward trend to the regression line in the central region but diverges slightly at the extremes, suggesting the relationship is roughly linear but may be pulled by those outlying observations.
Overall, the scatterplot confirms that income has a positive partial association with 1981 water consumption even after controlling for education and prior water use habits.

1.4 Estimate a bivariate regression model of the water residuals on the income residuals and compare its estimate slope coefficient against the slope coefficient for income of the multiple model from task 1.1. Why are you allowed to suppress the intercept in this model? [0.5 point]

Your answer goes here


answer 1-4

## Bivariate Regression of Residuals
## Suppress intercept (-1) because residual means are zero
## Compare slope with income coefficient from mod1.lm (Task 1.1)
summary(lm(e.water81 ~ e.income - 1))                  # suppress intercept: residual means = 0


Call:
lm(formula = e.water81 ~ e.income - 1)

Residuals:
    Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
-4635.2  -473.4   -65.0   405.6  4831.1 

Coefficients:
         Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
e.income   24.717      3.538   6.985 9.21e-12 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 912.2 on 495 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.08973,   Adjusted R-squared:  0.08789 
F-statistic: 48.79 on 1 and 495 DF,  p-value: 9.213e-12

## Compare coefficients
cat("Slope from residual regression:        ", 
    coef(lm(e.water81 ~ e.income - 1))["e.income"], "\n")

Slope from residual regression:         24.71652 

cat("income coefficient from full model:    ", 
    coef(mod1.lm)["income"], "\n")

income coefficient from full model:     24.71652 


The bivariate regression of the water residuals on the income residuals (with the intercept suppressed) produces a slope coefficient of 24.717, which is virtually identical to the income coefficient of 24.717 from the full multiple regression model in Task 1.1. The two values match exactly up to rounding, which confirms the key theoretical point of this exercise.
This result demonstrates that the partial regression coefficient of income in the multiple model is mathematically equivalent to the slope obtained by regressing the residuals of water81 on the residuals of income, after both variables have had the linear effects of educat and water80 removed. In other words, OLS automatically “cleans out” the influence of the other independent variables when estimating each partial coefficient.
Note: the reasons we were allowed to suppress the intercept:
We are allowed to suppress the intercept because the residuals from any OLS regression always have a mean of exactly zero by construction. Since both e.water81 and e.income have means of zero, the regression line must pass through the origin (0, 0). Including an intercept would be redundant — it would simply estimate to approximately zero anyway. Therefore, forcing the intercept to zero with - 1 is not only justified but actually the more correct specification for this particular model.


Task 2: A Multiple Regression Model with Factors and Partial F-test [5 points]
Use the dBase data file PROVINCES.DBF (read it into R with the function foreign::read.dbf)
You will experiment with regression models that aim at explaining the 1994 total fertility rate TOTFERTRAT (number of children born by a woman during her lifetime) within the 95 Italian provinces. The following independent variables are: [a] the metric illiteracy rate ILLITERRAT, [b] the metric average woman’s age at first marriage FEMMARAGE, [c] the metric divorce rate DIVORCERAT, [d] the metric televisions per household TELEPERFAM and [e] a regional factor REGION denoting whether a province is located on the islands of Sicily or Sardinia, or in the southern, central or northern parts of Italy’s mainland.
Note: please do not perform variable transformations in the task.
2.1 Use common sense arguments how these four metric variables will influence the provincial fertility rates. Use one or two sentences per explanation and formulate preferably one sided null and alternative hypotheses based on your explanation. The statistical hypotheses should be type-set properly, for instance, as \(H_0:\beta_{VarXYZ}\leq0\) against \(H_1:\beta_{VarXYZ}>0\). Format everything in the table shown below. [0.5 points]


answer 2-1








Variable
Common Sense Arguments
Statistical Hypotheses




ILLITERRAT
Provinces with higher illiteracy rates tend to have lower levels of education, which is often associated with less awareness of family planning and fewer economic opportunities for women. Therefore, we expect illiteracy to be positively related to fertility rates.
\(H_0: \beta_{ILLITERRAT} \leq 0\) against \(H_1: \beta_{ILLITERRAT} > 0\)


FEMMARAGE
When women marry at an older age, they have fewer remaining years in their reproductive window, which naturally leads to having fewer children on average. So we expect a negative relationship between age at first marriage and fertility.
\(H_0: \beta_{FEMMARAGE} \geq 0\) against \(H_1: \beta_{FEMMARAGE} < 0\)


DIVORCERAT
A higher divorce rate may reflect less stable family structures and a more individualistic lifestyle, both of which tend to discourage having large families. We therefore expect divorce rate to be negatively associated with fertility.
\(H_0: \beta_{DIVORCERAT} \geq 0\) against \(H_1: \beta_{DIVORCERAT} < 0\)


TELEPERFAM
Households with more televisions are likely wealthier and more exposed to modern lifestyles and values (such as smaller family norms portrayed in media), which tends to reduce fertility. We therefore expect a negative relationship.
\(H_0: \beta_{TELEPERFAM} \geq 0\) against \(H_1: \beta_{TELEPERFAM} < 0\)




2.2 Generate a scatterplot matrix showing the dependent variable and the four metric independent variables. Also generate a boxplot of the fertility rate against the factor variable REGION. Briefly interpret both plots. [0.75 points]

Your answer goes here


answer 2-2

## Read in the PROVINCES.DBF data file
provinces <- foreign::read.dbf("PROVINCES.DBF")

## Scatterplot matrix: dependent variable + four metric independent variables
##  Exclude factor variable from scatterplotMatrix
car::scatterplotMatrix(
  ~TOTFERTRAT+ILLITERRAT+FEMMARAGE+DIVORCERAT+TELEPERFAM,
  data=provinces,
  diagonal=list(method="density"),
  smooth=list(span=0.5,
              lty.smooth=1,
              col.smooth="red"),
  regLine=list(col="green"),
  main="Scatterplot Matrix: Fertility Rate and Metric Predictors"
)







## Boxplot of fertility rate against the factor variable REGION
## Ref: Chapt03a.r line 66 -- evaluate factor variable in boxplot
boxplot(TOTFERTRAT~REGION, data=provinces,
        xlab="Region", ylab="Total Fertility Rate",
        main="Fertility Rate by Region",
        col="lightblue")








Scatterplot Matrix Interpretation:
From the scatterplot matrix, we can see that TOTFERTRAT has a fairly clear positive relationship with ILLITERRAT — provinces with higher illiteracy rates tend to have higher fertility rates. On the other hand, FEMMARAGE, DIVORCERAT, and TELEPERFAM all show negative relationships with TOTFERTRAT, meaning that as these variables increase, the fertility rate tends to go down. These patterns are generally consistent with what we expected from our common sense arguments in task 2.1. It is also worth noting that several predictors are correlated with each other — for example, ILLITERRAT seems negatively related to both FEMMARAGE and TELEPERFAM, which suggests that multicollinearity could become an issue in the regression model.
Boxplot Interpretation:
The boxplot shows quite noticeable differences in fertility rates across the five Italian regions. Sicily and the South have clearly higher median fertility rates (around 1.55 and 1.40 respectively) compared to Central Italy, the North, and Sardinia, which all cluster around 1.0 to 1.1. This suggests that geographic location plays an important role in explaining fertility rate variation, and that the REGION factor is likely to be a meaningful predictor in our regression model.

2.3 Run a base model multiple regression with the four metric variables to explain the variation of the fertility rates. Interpret this model [a] in the light of your earlier stated hypotheses in task 2.1, [b] the significances of the estimate regression coefficients and [c] the goodness of fit. [0.75 points]

Your answer goes here


answer 2-3

## Base model: multiple regression with four metric variables only
mod2.lm <- lm(TOTFERTRAT ~ ILLITERRAT+FEMMARAGE+DIVORCERAT+TELEPERFAM, data=provinces)
summary(mod2.lm)


Call:
lm(formula = TOTFERTRAT ~ ILLITERRAT + FEMMARAGE + DIVORCERAT + 
    TELEPERFAM, data = provinces)

Residuals:
     Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
-0.21906 -0.06267 -0.00966  0.05425  0.41272 

Coefficients:
             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)  4.496337   0.513726   8.752 1.13e-13 ***
ILLITERRAT   0.020377   0.008735   2.333   0.0219 *  
FEMMARAGE   -0.088837   0.020771  -4.277 4.71e-05 ***
DIVORCERAT  -0.112265   0.055648  -2.017   0.0466 *  
TELEPERFAM  -1.226364   0.183037  -6.700 1.76e-09 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 0.1035 on 90 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.8096,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.8012 
F-statistic: 95.69 on 4 and 90 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16

cbind(Estimate=coef(mod2.lm), confint(mod2.lm))     # print confidence intervals for parameter estimates

               Estimate       2.5 %       97.5 %
(Intercept)  4.49633724  3.47573155  5.516942917
ILLITERRAT   0.02037729  0.00302309  0.037731490
FEMMARAGE   -0.08883705 -0.13010151 -0.047572580
DIVORCERAT  -0.11226497 -0.22282020 -0.001709743
TELEPERFAM  -1.22636404 -1.58999801 -0.862730064


[a] Hypotheses from Task 2.1:
All four variables show coefficient signs that match our expectations from task 2.1. ILLITERRAT has a positive coefficient (0.0204), consistent with our hypothesis \(H_1: \beta_{ILLITERRAT} > 0\). The remaining three variables — FEMMARAGE (−0.0888), DIVORCERAT (−0.1123), and TELEPERFAM (−1.2264) — all have negative coefficients, which is exactly what we predicted. So overall, the direction of all four effects agrees with our common sense arguments.
[b] Significance of the Regression Coefficients:
All four predictors are statistically significant at the 5% level. TELEPERFAM is by far the most significant variable (\(t = -6.70\), \(p < 0.001\)), followed by FEMMARAGE (\(t = -4.28\), \(p < 0.001\)). Both ILLITERRAT (\(t = 2.33\), \(p = 0.022\)) and DIVORCERAT (\(t = -2.02\), \(p = 0.047\)) are significant at the 5% level but are relatively weaker. The confidence intervals for all four coefficients also do not include zero, which further confirms their significance.
[c] Goodness of Fit:
The model fits the data quite well. The adjusted \(R^2 = 0.801\) tells us that about 80% of the variation in provincial fertility rates is explained by these four metric variables alone, which is quite impressive for a social science dataset. The overall F-statistic is \(F(4, 90) = 95.69\) with \(p < 0.001\), strongly rejecting the null hypothesis that all slope coefficients are simultaneously zero. The residual standard error is 0.1035, meaning our predictions are typically off by about 0.10 children per woman.

2.4 Calculate the standardized beta-coefficients for the multiple model form task 2.3. Rank the independent variables according to the absolute strength of their effects on the fertility rates and plot the beta coefficients with the coefplot( ) function. Use proper options for the coefplot( ) function. [0.75 points]

Your answer goes here


answer 2-4

## Beta weights (standardized coefficients) for mod2.lm

provincesNew   <- provinces[ ,sapply(provinces,is.numeric)]       # Remove non-numeric variables
provincesScale <- as.data.frame(scale(provincesNew))              # apply z-transformation with scale function
                                                                   # dataframe provincesScale holds the transformed values
mod3.lm <- lm(TOTFERTRAT ~ -1+ILLITERRAT+FEMMARAGE+DIVORCERAT+TELEPERFAM, 
              data=provincesScale)
summary(mod3.lm)                                                   # display beta-values.


Call:
lm(formula = TOTFERTRAT ~ -1 + ILLITERRAT + FEMMARAGE + DIVORCERAT + 
    TELEPERFAM, data = provincesScale)

Residuals:
     Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
-0.94379 -0.27001 -0.04162  0.23371  1.77813 

Coefficients:
           Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
ILLITERRAT  0.17303    0.07377   2.346   0.0212 *  
FEMMARAGE  -0.30905    0.07186  -4.301 4.27e-05 ***
DIVORCERAT -0.12638    0.06230  -2.029   0.0454 *  
TELEPERFAM -0.44537    0.06611  -6.737 1.43e-09 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 0.4434 on 91 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.8096,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.8013 
F-statistic: 96.75 on 4 and 91 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16

## Coefficient plot useful for beta weights because the parameters are on the same scale
library(coefplot)

Warning: package 'coefplot' was built under R version 4.5.2


Loading required package: ggplot2


Warning: package 'ggplot2' was built under R version 4.5.3

coefplot(mod3.lm, sort="magnitude",
         xlab="Standardized Beta Coefficient",

         title="Beta Coefficients: Base Model (Task 2.3)")

TELEPERFAM has the strongest effect on fertility rates with a beta coefficient of −0.445, meaning that a one standard deviation increase in televisions per household is associated with a 0.445 standard deviation decrease in fertility rate — making it by far the most influential predictor in this model. FEMMARAGE comes in second (−0.309), suggesting that women marrying later also has a fairly strong suppressing effect on fertility. ILLITERRAT ranks third (0.173) with a positive effect, while DIVORCERAT has the weakest influence (−0.126) among the four variables.
The coefplot confirms this ranking visually — TELEPERFAM sits furthest to the left of the zero line, while DIVORCERAT has a confidence interval that barely stays on the negative side, reflecting its borderline significance we already saw in task 2.3.

2.5 Run five separate regressions on the [a] independent metric variables as well as [b] the dependent fertility rate using the factor REGION as independent variable.
Does the REGION factor explain variation of the four independent variables as well as the fertility rate, i.e., what are the \(R^2\)’s? [0.75 points]
Hint: To calibrate all five models with one function call you can use the regression formula syntax cbind(TOTFERTRAT,ILLITERRAT,FEMMARAGE,DIVORCERAT,TELEPERFAM)~REGION. The summary function gives you the results for all five models.

The results show that REGION does a fairly good job of explaining the variation in all five variables. All five models have statistically significant overall F-tests (\(p < 0.001\)), indicating that the REGION factor as a whole explains a nontrivial share of variation in each response variable.
The \(R^2\) values are summarized below:








Response Variable
\(R^2\)
Interpretation




TOTFERTRAT
0.741
REGION explains 74.1% of fertility rate variation


ILLITERRAT
0.749
REGION explains 74.9% of illiteracy rate variation


FEMMARAGE
0.629
REGION explains 62.9% of female marriage age variation


DIVORCERAT
0.423
REGION explains 42.3% of divorce rate variation


TELEPERFAM
0.531
REGION explains 53.1% of televisions per household variation



These results are quite revealing. REGION explains a large share of the variation not only in the dependent variable TOTFERTRAT (\(R^2 = 0.741\)), but also in the metric predictors themselves — especially ILLITERRAT (\(R^2 = 0.749\)) and FEMMARAGE (\(R^2 = 0.629\)). This tells us that where a province is located geographically is strongly associated with its socioeconomic characteristics.
Looking at the individual coefficients, Sicily and the South consistently stand out across all five models — they have significantly higher illiteracy rates, lower female marriage ages, fewer divorces, and fewer televisions compared to the Central reference group, which all naturally links back to their higher fertility rates.
This overlap between REGION and the metric predictors is actually an important warning sign — it suggests that when we add REGION into the regression model alongside the metric variables in task 2.6, multicollinearity could become a problem, because REGION is essentially capturing much of the same information that ILLITERRAT, FEMMARAGE, and TELEPERFAM are already measuring.

2.6 Run the multiple regression model with the four metric variables plus the REGION factor to explain the variation of the fertility rates. Speculate in an informed way why some independent metric variables are no longer significant? Does the factor REGION improve the interpretability of the model or makes it messy? [0.75 points]

Your answer goes here


answer 2-6

## Full model: four metric variables plus REGION factor
mod26.lm <- lm(TOTFERTRAT ~ ILLITERRAT+FEMMARAGE+DIVORCERAT+TELEPERFAM+REGION, 
              data=provinces)
summary(mod26.lm)

Regression Results:
After adding REGION into the model, the adjusted \(R^2\) increases slightly from 0.801 (task 2.3) to 0.829, suggesting a modest improvement in model fit. However, looking at the individual coefficients, something interesting happens — two variables that were significant in the base model are no longer significant here:

ILLITERRAT: was significant (\(p = 0.022\)) → now completely insignificant (\(p = 0.881\))
DIVORCERAT: was significant (\(p = 0.047\)) → now insignificant (\(p = 0.123\))

Why did this happen?
This is actually not surprising when we connect back to our findings in task 2.5. We found that REGION already explains about 74.9% of the variation in ILLITERRAT and 42.3% of the variation in DIVORCERAT. This means that REGION and these metric variables are carrying a lot of overlapping information — once REGION is in the model, it has already “absorbed” much of what ILLITERRAT and DIVORCERAT were trying to explain, leaving them with very little additional explanatory power. This is a classic sign of multicollinearity between the factor and the metric predictors.
Does REGION improve interpretability or make the model messy?
In my opinion, adding REGION makes the model messier rather than more interpretable. While Sicily and South remain significantly different from the Central reference group, the North and Sardinia are not significant at all — meaning the factor is only partially useful. More importantly, the metric variables that were telling us something meaningful about why fertility differs (e.g., illiteracy, divorce behavior) are now being overshadowed by the geographic label. The base model in task 2.3 was actually cleaner and easier to interpret, because each variable had a clear and significant role to play.

2.7 Use a partial F-test to check whether the model in task 2.6 has improved the model fit of the base model in task 2.3 significantly. [0.75 points]
That is, test the null hypothesis: \(H_0:\beta_{Region1}=\beta_{Region2}=...=\beta_{RegionJ}=0\) against the alternative hypothesis is \(H_0:\beta_{Regionj}\neq0\) for at least one \(j\in\{1,2,...,J\}\).

Your answer goes here


answer 2-7

Anova(mod26.lm)              # Type II ANOVA: equivalent to t-test for individual variables

Anova Table (Type II tests)

Response: TOTFERTRAT
            Sum Sq Df F value    Pr(>F)    
ILLITERRAT 0.00021  1  0.0224  0.881362    
FEMMARAGE  0.06147  1  6.6847  0.011409 *  
DIVORCERAT 0.02233  1  2.4288  0.122795    
TELEPERFAM 0.28298  1 30.7747 3.145e-07 ***

Residuals  0.79079 86

Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1


Partial F-test Results:
The anova() function compares the restricted model (task 2.3, without REGION) against the full model (task 2.6, with REGION). The results show:
\[F(4, 86) = 4.711, \quad p = 0.00174\]
Since the p-value is 0.00174, which is well below the 5% significance level, we reject the null hypothesis \(H_0: \beta_{Region1} = \beta_{Region2} = ... = \beta_{RegionJ} = 0\). This means that at least one of the REGION coefficients is significantly different from zero, and adding REGION into the model does statistically significantly improve the model fit compared to the base model.
The Anova() Type II test further confirms this — REGION as a whole factor has \(F(4, 86) = 4.711\) with \(p = 0.00174\), which is consistent with the partial F-test result. We can also see that TELEPERFAM remains the strongest individual predictor (\(F = 30.77\), \(p < 0.001\)), while ILLITERRAT (\(p = 0.881\)) and DIVORCERAT (\(p = 0.123\)) remain insignificant even in this Type II framework.
Overall Conclusion:
The partial F-test tells us that REGION adds statistically significant explanatory power beyond the four metric variables. However, this comes at a cost — as we discussed in task 2.6, due to substantial shared variance between REGION and the metric predictors, raising concerns about potential multicollinearity. So while the improvement in fit is statistically real (the RSS drops from 0.964 to 0.791), we need to weigh this against the reduced interpretability of the model. In practice, whether to keep REGION in the model depends on whether geographic context is theoretically important for our research question.


Task 3: Identification of the Underlying Model Structure [1 point]
Use the five data-frames df_1 to df_5 in the workspace InteractionWithDummy.RData for this task (the workspace can be imported with the load( ) function). These data-frames comprise of three variables: y for the dependent variables, d for a binary dummy variable, and x for a metric variable. Each of these data-frames is best described by one of these competing models:



Name
Models Structure




Full interation model
mod.full <- lm(y~d*x, data=df)


Different intercept model
mod.int <- lm(y~d+x, data=df)


Different slope model
mod.slope <- lm(y~d:x, data=df)


Just different means model
mod.means <- lm(y~d, data=df)


Plain bivariate model
mod.plain <- lm(y~x, data=df)



For each of the data-frames generate an informative scatterplot showing the regression regimes for both groups. You can employ the syntax car::scatterplot(y~x|d, data=df). Then identify by using just visual inspection, which of the competing model structures best describes the given datasets.
Note: Each relationship in df_1 to df_5 is based on a modest degree of induced randomness. However, the general pattern is still clearly identifiable.
3.1 Plot the data and identify the underlying model structure for df_1. [0.2 points]

Your answer goes here

answer 3-1

library(car)

load("InteractionWithDummy.RData")     # load df_1 to df_5 into workspace


## Scatterplot and Model Identification for df_1
car::scatterplot(y ~ x | d, data = df_1,
                 smooth=F, 
                 xlab  = "x (metric variable)",
                 ylab  = "y (dependent variable)",
                 main  = "df_1: Scatterplot by Dummy Group (d)")








The scatterplot for df_1 shows two distinct groups separated by the dummy variable d. Both groups (d = 0 in blue circles and d = 1 in pink triangles) display a clear positive relationship between x and y. Importantly, the two regression lines appear to run parallel to each other — they have approximately the same slope but clearly different intercepts, with the d = 1 group consistently sitting higher than the d = 0 group across the entire range of x.
This pattern is best described by the different intercept model:
mod.int <- lm(y ~ d + x, data = df_1)
In this model, the dummy variable d shifts the regression line up or down (intercept effect), while the slope of x remains the same for both groups.

3.2 Plot the data and identify the underlying model structure for df_2. [0.2 points]

Your answer goes here


answer 3-2

## Scatterplot and Model Identification for df_2
car::scatterplot(y ~ x | d, data = df_2,
                 smooth=F, 
                 xlab  = "x (metric variable)",
                 ylab  = "y (dependent variable)",
                 main  = "df_2: Scatterplot by Dummy Group (d)")








The scatterplot for df_2 shows two distinct groups, separated by the dummy variable d. Both groups (d = 0 in blue circles and d = 1 in pink triangles) show a clear positive relationship between x and y. Notably, the two regression lines seem to share the same intercept at approximately 2, but they have distinctly different slopes, with the d = 1 group exhibiting a steeper slope than the d = 0 group.
This pattern is best described by the different slope model:
mod.slope <- lm(y ~ d:x, data = df_2)
In this model, the dummy variable d splits the regression into two different slopes, while the intercept remains the same for both groups.

3.3 Plot the data and identify the underlying model structure for df_3. [0.2 points]

Your answer goes here


answer 3-3

## Scatterplot and Model Identification for df_3
car::scatterplot(y ~ x | d, data = df_3,
                 smooth=F, 
                 xlab  = "x (metric variable)",
                 ylab  = "y (dependent variable)",
                 main  = "df_3: Scatterplot by Dummy Group (d)")








The scatterplot for df_3 shows two groups separated by the dummy variable d. Both groups (d = 0 in blue circles and d = 1 in pink triangles) display a clear positive relationship between x and y. The two regression lines appear to share the same intercept and the same slope, making them nearly overlap completely on the plot. This suggests that the dummy variable d has no meaningful effect on y.
This pattern is best described by the plain bivariate model:
mod.plain <- lm(y ~ x, data = df_3)
In this model, only x drives the variation in y, and the grouping by d does not add any explanatory power.

3.4 Plot the data and identify the underlying model structure for df_4. [0.2 points]

Your answer goes here


answer 3-4

## Scatterplot and Model Identification for df_4
car::scatterplot(y ~ x | d, data = df_4,
                 smooth=F, 
                 xlab  = "x (metric variable)",
                 ylab  = "y (dependent variable)",
                 main  = "df_4: Scatterplot by Dummy Group (d)")








The scatterplot for df_4 shows two distinct groups separated by the dummy variable d. Both groups (d = 0 in blue circles and d = 1 in pink triangles) display approximately horizontal regression lines, indicating that x has no meaningful effect on y. The d = 0 group stays consistently lower while the d = 1 group stays consistently higher, suggesting only a difference in group means.
This pattern is best described by the just different means model:
mod.means <- lm(y ~ d, data = df_4)
In this model, the dummy variable d divides the data into two groups, and the regression lines simply represent the group means of y for each group. The metric variable x does not contribute any explanatory power.

3.5 Plot the data and identify the underlying model structure for df_5. [0.2 points]

Your answer goes here


answer 3-5

## Scatterplot and Model Identification for df_5
car::scatterplot(y ~ x | d, data = df_5,
                 smooth=F, 
                 xlab  = "x (metric variable)",
                 ylab  = "y (dependent variable)",
                 main  = "df_5: Scatterplot by Dummy Group (d)")








The scatterplot for df_5 shows two distinct groups separated by the dummy variable d. The d = 0 group (blue circles) displays a steep positive slope, while the d = 1 group (pink triangles) shows a nearly horizontal line around y = 3. Importantly, the two regression lines have both different intercepts — the blue line starts much lower at x = 0 — and different slopes, with the blue line rising sharply while the pink line remains almost flat.
This pattern is best described by the full interaction model:
mod.full <- lm(y ~ d*x, data = df_5)
In this model, the dummy variable d affects both the intercept and the slope simultaneously, allowing each group to have a completely independent regression line. This is the most flexible model among the five competing structures.

title: Week11: Hamilton Chapter 7 - Logistic Regression Analysis
text:
The dependent variable is categorical (contrast to OLS, which assumes a continuous distribution).
The independent variables can be any mix of metric variables and categorical variables (factors) as well as their interaction terms.
Classification based on
[a] the number of categories of the response variable and
[b] whether each observation \(i\) constitutes a single records (binary response) with \(n_i = 1\) or several observations are grouped together into aggregates (rates based on group counts \(n_g\)).
Here each group member must share the same exogenous group attributes:










Two Categories (dichotomous)
Multiple Categories (polytomous)




Individual Observations
Binary distribution
Binary multinomial distribution


Grouped Observations
Binomial distribution
Multinomial distribution




There are specialized specifications of ordinal scaled dependent variables available. See the proportional odds model in Fox & Weisberg
Basic logistic regression focuses on the simple case with individual observations, and each observation can fall into just one of two mutually exclusive categories.
The category status is coded binary:

\[Y_i = \begin{cases}
1 & \text{observation } i \text{ in first category} \\
0 & \text{observation } i \text{ in second category}
\end{cases}\]

title: Week11: Hamilton Chapter 7 - Logistic Regression Analysis
section: 2.1 Problem 1: Linear Predictions outside the feasible range
text:
2.1 Problem 1: Linear Predictions outside the feasible range

The predicted value cannot be interpreted as probability

\[\hat{\pi}_i = \Pr(Y_i = 1 | x_i) = b_0 + b_1 \cdot x_i \quad \text{for a given exogenous } x_i\]


The predicted linear probability value can fall outside the feasible range of probabilities \([0, 1]\).
For example: see HAM Fig 7.4 when years lived in town is greater than 70.

title: Week11: Hamilton Chapter 7 - Logistic Regression Analysis
section: 2.2 Solution to Problem 1: Infeasible Range of Predictions
text:
2.2 Solution to Problem 1: Infeasible Range of Predictions

Calculate odds \(\mathcal{O}_i = \frac{\pi_i}{1-\pi_i}\) with value range \(\mathcal{O}_i \in [0, \infty]\).
Transform odds into logits \(\mathcal{L}_i = \ln(\mathcal{O}_i) = \ln\left(\frac{\pi_i}{1-\pi_i}\right)\) with the value range \(\mathcal{L}_i \in [-\infty, +\infty]\).
\(\Rightarrow\) the logits can be modeled by a linear function!



Note: This specification only works for probabilities excluding certain events. Those probabilities are either 0 or 1.
This means, it is impossible to transform revealed outcomes \(Y_i = 1\) or \(Y_i = 0\) directly into odds or logits.
The linear function in the logits becomes (here for the i-th observation):

\[\ln\left(\frac{\pi_i}{1-\pi_i}\right) = \mathcal{L}_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot x_{i,1} + \cdots + \beta_{K-1} \cdot x_{i,K-1}\]

The estimated probabilities are given by the inverse of the logit function:

\[\ln\left(\frac{\pi_i}{1-\pi_i}\right) = \mathcal{L}_i\]
\[\Rightarrow \pi = \frac{1}{1 + \exp(-\mathcal{L}_i)}\]
\[\Leftrightarrow \pi = \frac{\exp(\mathcal{L}_i)}{1 + \exp(\mathcal{L}_i)} \quad \text{(equivalent expression)}\]

The inverse logit function gives the logistic curve displayed in HAM Fig 7.3.
For bivariate logistic regression this curve is monotonically increasing for a positive “slope” parameter \(\beta_1\) and monotonically decreasing for a negative “slope” parameter \(\beta_1\) (HAM Fig 7.4)
See also the R-script FunctionalProbForms.r.

title: Week11: Hamilton Chapter 7 - Logistic Regression Analysis
text:
2.3 Problem 2: Heteroscedasticity of disturbances

Residuals for each observation \(Y_i\) can take only two distinct values:

\[e_i = \begin{cases}
1 - \hat{\pi}_i & \text{if } Y_i = 1 \\
0 - \hat{\pi}_i & \text{if } Y_i = 0
\end{cases}\]

Following the binary distribution, the variance of the population disturbances is given by:

\[Var(\varepsilon_i) = (1-\pi_i) \cdot \pi_i\]

The spread of the disturbances depends on the varying true probabilities of the individual observations \(i\) and, therefore, their variances become heteroscedastic.

title: Week11: Hamilton Chapter 7 - Logistic Regression Analysis
text:
2.4 Solution to the heteroscedasticity problem

Maximum likelihood estimation automatically accounts for the heteroscedasticity.
Alternatively, one could us iteratively re-weighted least squares (see Hamilton note 10 on page 238 and page 247).
However, the weights must be given in terms of logits \(\mathcal{L}_i\) for which the variance is at the \(k^{th}\) iteration (here \(n_i = 1\)):

\[Var\left(\ln\frac{Y_i}{1-Y_i}\right) = \frac{1}{n_i \cdot \hat{\pi}_i \cdot (1-\hat{\pi}_i)}\]

Note: In contrast, the variance of the proportion estimate \(Y_i = \frac{\#\ of\ successes}{n_i}\) is

\[Var(Y_i) = \frac{\hat{\pi}_i \cdot (1-\hat{\pi}_i)}{n_i}\]

title: Week11: Hamilton Chapter 7 - Logistic Regression Analysis
section: 7.1 [a] Approach in terms of the logit
text:
7.1 [a] Approach in terms of the logit

Recall that the logit-transformation is a monotone function (the larger the logit the larger the underlying probability).
Thus, the estimated parameters in \(\hat{\mathcal{L}}_i = b_0 + b_1 \cdot x_{i,1} + \cdots + b_{K-1} \cdot x_{i,K-1}\) can be interpreted as:
for positive coefficients \(b_k\), the greater X the larger the expected probability. Analog for negative \(b_k\)s.
Individual confidence intervals can be calculated for the logit specification:

\[b_k - t_\alpha(SE_{b_k}) \leq \beta_k \leq b_k + t_\alpha(SE_{b_k})\]
with \(t_\alpha(SE_{b_k})\) being a function of the standard error of coefficient \(b_k\) and the significance level.


We can reject the null hypothesis \(H_0: \beta_k = 0\) if the interval does not cover the value zero.
Interpretation of the model in terms of the logits is only the starting point because it does provide only an indirect link to the more meaningful probabilities.

title: Week11: Hamilton Chapter 7 - Logistic Regression Analysis
section: 7.2 [b] Approach in terms of the odds
text:
7.2 [b] Approach in terms of the odds

The odds-specification can be written as

\[\frac{\hat{\pi}_i}{1-\hat{\pi}_i} = e^{b_0} \cdot e^{b_1 \cdot x_{i1}} \cdot \ldots \cdot e^{b_{K-1} \cdot x_{i(K-1)}}\]
\[= e^{b_0} \cdot \left(e^{b_1}\right)^{x_{i1}} \cdot \ldots \cdot \left(e^{b_{K-1}}\right)^{x_{i(K-1)}}\]



Assuming all variables remain at a preset level except for the \(k^{th}\) variable, then one unit change in \(x_{ik}\) changes the odds of observing \(Y_i = 1\) by the factor \(e^{b_k}\) (multiplicative link).
If the estimated parameter is zero, i.e., \(b_k = 0\), then the odds won’t change because \(\left(e^{0}\right)^{x_{ik}} = 1\) (one is the neutral factor in multiplication)
The confidence interval in terms of the exponential function becomes

\[\exp\left(b_k - t(SE_{b_k})\right) \leq \exp(\beta_k) \leq \exp\left(b_k + t(SE_{b_k})\right)\]
If it does not include 1 the null hypothesis then \(H_0: \exp(\beta_k) = 1\) can be rejected.

The percentage change in the odds for observing \(Y_i = 1\) is given by \(100 \cdot \left(e^{b_k} - 1\right)\).
For an indicator variable \(X\) this has a clear interpretation by the odds-ratios:

\[OR = \frac{\hat{\mathcal{O}}_{X=1}}{\hat{\mathcal{O}}_{X=0}} = e^{b_k}\]
because the effects of the remaining variables cancel out irrespectively of their observed levels:
\[OR = \frac{e^{b_0} \cdot e^{b_1 \cdot x_{i1}} \cdot \ldots \cdot e^{b_k \cdot 1} \cdot \ldots \cdot e^{b_{K-1} \cdot x_{i(K-1)}}}{e^{b_0} \cdot e^{b_1 \cdot x_{i1}} \cdot \ldots \cdot \underbrace{e^{b_k \cdot 0}}_{=1} \cdot \ldots \cdot e^{b_{K-1} \cdot x_{i(K-1)}}} = e^{b_k}\]

title: Week11: Hamilton Chapter 7 - Logistic Regression Analysis
section: 7.3 [c] Approach in terms of the probabilities
text:
7.3 [c] Approach in terms of the probabilities

The estimated probability for observation i can is expressed by:

\[\hat{\pi}_i = \frac{1}{1 + \exp(-\mathcal{L}_i)} = \frac{1}{1 + \exp(-\mathbf{x}_i^T \cdot \mathbf{b})}\]

The interpretation that one-unit change in \(X\) causes \(b\) unit changes in the probability (either additively or multiplicatively) is no longer valid.
It dependents on the relative value of the predicted probabilities \(\hat{\pi}_i\) (see HAM Fig 7.3):
The slope of the logistic curve for a given probability \(\hat{\pi}_i\) with respect to one unit change in an independent variable \(X\) is given by \(b_k \cdot \hat{\pi}_i \cdot (1 - \hat{\pi}_i)\)








\(\hat{\pi}_i\)
\(slope = b_k \cdot \hat{\pi}_i \cdot (1-\hat{\pi}_i)\)




0.01
\(b_k \times 0.0099\)


0.05
\(b_k \times 0.04575\)


0.10
\(b_k \times 0.09\)


0.50
\(b_k \times 0.25\)


0.90
\(b_k \times 0.09\)


0.95
\(b_k \times 0.04575\)


0.99
\(b_k \times 0.0099\)




It has it maximum at \(\hat{\pi}_i = 0.5\)
The logistic curve fairly linear in the short intervals of \(\pi \in [0 < \pi^{lower}, \pi^{upper} < 1]\).
Consequently, weighted linear regression can be used for observed rates, as long as they don’t vary too much.
For metric variables only conditional effect plots, where the remaining variables are set at a specific fixed level, can be given (see HAM Figs 7.5 and 7.6)




Note the identity of the logistic curve in Fig 7.4 (model with the only variable “lived”) and the logistic curve of the “average” respondent in Fig 7.5.
This allows investigating the effects of one variable on the probability for different scenarios (in the example HAM p 231: population segment of risk-takers against cautious people).

title: Week08: Maximum Likelihood Principle (Abridged)
text:
Besides ordinary least squares estimation principle, there are several alternative estimation principles such as the method of moments, maximum likelihood or Bayesian used in statistics. Each of these estimation methods employs some form of optimization techniques to estimate the unknown parameters from observed (or hypothetical) sample data.
Historically, the maximum likelihood and OLS estimators were (and still are) the most prominent estimation methods.
However, other principles are gaining momentum under specific conditions.
The maximum likelihood estimator explicitly requires:

that the functional form of the underlying distribution of each random variable is explicitly known and that the given sample data conform with this distribution (the parameters of the underlying distribution do not need to be fully specified),
that the observations are statistically independent and identically distributed (or can be transformed to satisfy the i.i.d. requirement).

Recall: OLS is more relaxed: it satisfies the Gauss-Markov theorem (unbiasedness and efficiency estimator even if some assumptions are not perfectly satisfied) without relying on strict distributional assumptions.

title: Week08: Maximum Likelihood Principle (Abridged)
section: 2.1 Example of the maximum likelihood principal
text:
2.1 Example of the maximum likelihood principal

Flipping a coin n=10 times with the given outcome HHTHHHTTHH. Each throw is conducted independently of the other throws.
Our intuitive guess would be \(\Pr(H) = \frac{\text{# of heads}}{\text{# of trails}} = 0.7\)
The probability of obtaining this sequence of observations, prior to collecting the data, is a function of the unknown population parameter \(\pi\):

\[\Pr(\text{data} | \text{parameter}) = \Pr(HHTHHHTTHH | \pi)\]
\[= \pi \cdot \pi \cdot (1-\pi) \cdot \pi \cdot \pi \cdot \pi \cdot (1-\pi) \cdot (1-\pi) \cdot \pi \cdot \pi\]
\[= \pi^7 \cdot (1-\pi)^3\]

That is, each throw is binary distributed and its outcome is independent of the other throws. Therefore, the product of individual probabilities gives the join-probability.

Assuming the data are given and the unknown random parameter is \(\pi\) then the conditional probability \(\Pr(\text{data} | \pi)\) changes into a likelihood function:

\[L(\text{unknown parameter} | \text{observed data}) = L(\pi | HHTHHHTTHH) = \pi^7 \cdot (1-\pi)^{10-7}\]
Note: A likelihood function is not a proper statistical distribution function for the unknown parameter \(\pi\) because it does not integrate to one over the range \(\pi \in [0,1]\).
> L <- function(p) p^7*(1-p)^(10-7)
> integrate(L,0,1)
0.0007575758 with absolute error < 8.4e-18


In our imagination we can vary the parameter \(\pi\) within its feasible range \(\pi \in [0,1]\) and find the maximum value of the likelihood function for the given sample observations at a particular value \(\hat{\pi}\). See the R script likeBinom.R.



The likelihood that our given data comes from a population with \(\pi = \hat{\pi}\) is highest at \(\hat{\pi}\). This is our best guess for the unknown underlying population parameter \(\pi\).
See table in Fox’s Appendix and Figure D.15



Discussion: If the true probabilities were \(\pi = 0.0\) or \(\pi = 1.0\) (the sure events) then the observed sample would have been impossible to observe.
The maximum likelihood estimation method also allows to evaluate the covariance matrix among the estimated parameters \(\theta\) (see Fox’s appendix pages 92-100)

title: Week08: Maximum Likelihood Principle (Abridged)
text:
2.2 The Best Estimate for \(\pi\) (skipped)

\(L(\pi | \text{data}) = \pi^x \cdot (1-\pi)^{n-x}\)
\(\Rightarrow \log L(\pi | \text{data}) = l(\pi) = x \cdot \ln \pi + (n-x) \cdot \ln(1-\pi)\)
The following rules are applied to find the derivative of \(l(\pi)\):
[a] \(\frac{\partial \ln(\pi)}{\partial \pi} = \frac{1}{\pi}\) and
[b] the chain rule of derivatives \(\frac{\partial \ln(1-\pi)}{\partial \pi} = \frac{1}{1-\pi} \cdot (-1)\)
with \(\frac{\partial \ln(1-\pi)}{\partial (1-\pi)} = \frac{1}{1-\pi}\) and \(\frac{\partial (1-\pi)}{\partial \pi} = -1\).
Thus the first derivative of the log-likelihood with respect to \(\pi\) becomes

\[\frac{\partial l(\pi)}{\partial \pi} = \frac{x}{\pi} + (n-x) \cdot \frac{1}{1-\pi} \cdot (-1)\]
A maximum is obtained by setting the first derivative equal to zero and solving for the unknown parameter:
\[\frac{x}{\pi} - \frac{n-x}{1-\pi} \equiv 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{x}{\pi} = \frac{n-x}{1-\pi} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1-\pi}{\pi} = \frac{n-x}{x} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{\pi} - 1 = \frac{n}{x} - 1\]
which leads to \(\hat{\pi} = \frac{x}{n}\).

The maximum likelihood \(L\) is given at \(L(\hat{\pi} | \text{data}) = \left(\frac{x}{n}\right)^x \cdot \left(1 - \frac{x}{n}\right)^{n-x}\)

title: Week08: Maximum Likelihood Principle (Abridged)
text:
2.3 Estimation of the parameter’s variance \(\text{Var}(\hat{\pi})\) (skipped)

To obtain \(\text{Var}(\theta) = I^{-1}(\theta) = \left[-E\left(\frac{\partial^2 l(\theta)}{\partial \theta \cdot \partial \theta^T}\right)\right]^{-1}\) note that for the binomial distribution the expected value of the sum of the successes is equal to n-times the population probability of a single event, \(E[x] = n \cdot \pi\)
\(\frac{\partial^2 l(\pi)}{\partial^2 \pi} = \frac{\partial \left(\frac{\partial l(\pi)}{\partial \pi}\right)}{\partial \pi} = \frac{\partial \left[\frac{x}{\pi} - \frac{n-x}{1-\pi}\right]}{\partial \pi} = -\frac{x}{\pi^2} - \frac{n-x}{(1-\pi)^2}\)
Thus we get for the expectation (information matrix)

\[E\left[-\frac{x}{\pi^2} - \frac{n-x}{(1-\pi)^2}\right] = -\frac{n\pi}{\pi^2} - \frac{n - n\pi}{(1-\pi)^2} = -\frac{n}{\pi} - \frac{n}{1-\pi}\]
\[= -n \cdot \left(\frac{(1-\pi) + \pi}{\pi \cdot (1-\pi)}\right) = -\frac{n}{\pi \cdot (1-\pi)}\]
Substituting \(\hat{\pi}\), multiplying the expression by \(-1\) and taking the inverse gives the estimated variance:
\[\text{Var}(\pi) = \frac{\hat{\pi} \cdot (1-\hat{\pi})}{n}\]

title: Week08: Maximum Likelihood Principle (Abridged)
text:
3.1 Likelihood Ratio Test (LR)

The likelihood ratio test requires that the model is estimated (calibrated) twice:

first under the null hypothesis and
then under the alternative hypothesis.

Under the null hypothesis means that the parameters, which are tested, are set a priori to a given value, which in most cases is zero (that is, not included in the estimation procedure).
The test statistic becomes \(\chi_H^2 = -2(\ln(L_{K-H}) - \ln(L_K)) = -2 \cdot \ln\left(\frac{L_{K-H}}{L_K}\right)\) where

\(\ln(L_K)\) is the log-likelihood of the unrestricted model with K freely varying parameters
and \(\ln(L_{K-H})\) is the log-likelihood of the model \(H\) restricted parameters.

The number of restrictions (parameters not estimated) is H.
The relationship \(\ln(L_{K-H}) \leq \ln(L_K)\) holds because the restricted likelihood function is associated with a model that fit the data not as well as the full model.
The \(\chi_H^2\) test statistic is asymptotically \(\chi^2\)-distributed with H degrees for freedom under the null hypothesis that the H restricted parameters are irrelevant.
Large values of \(\chi_H^2\) indicate that the a priori restricted parameters are relevant and should vary freely (that is different from their assumed values under the null hypothesis). Therefore, they should also be estimated.
The underlying idea of the likelihood ratio test is similar to that of the partial F-test.

title: Week08: Maximum Likelihood Principle (Abridged)
text:
3.2 The Wald test

Under the Wald test the full model with all parameters is estimated and the included parameters are tested against their values under the null hypothesis.
It requires that we evaluate the parameter’s standard errors (derived from the inverse information matrix) at the unrestricted maximum likelihood value.
The Wald test is a substitute for the single t-test for the parameters in the model. It helps to decide which parameters to drop from the model.

title: Week08: Maximum Likelihood Principle (Abridged)
section: 3.3 The Score test (a.k.a. Lagrange multiplier test)
text:
3.3 The Score test (a.k.a. Lagrange multiplier test)

The Score test estimates the restricted model where the parameters under scrutiny are set a priori to a given value (in most cases zero).
The score test helps to decide which parameters should be estimated freely and added to a restricted model.

title: Week08: Maximum Likelihood Principle (Abridged)
text:
3.4 General Discussion

Critical values of the \(\chi^2\) distribution are used with the degrees of freedom equal to

the number of restricted parameters for the likelihood ratio test, and
one for the Wald-test and Score-test if just individual parameters are tested.

These tests are only valid asymptotically for large samples of statistically independent observations.
It can be shown that \(S \leq LR \leq W\) when they are applied under identical circumstances.
Thus, the larger Wald test will reject the null hypothesis more frequently than the other tests.
Visualization of the three test principles.

title: Week08: Maximum Likelihood Principle (Abridged)
text:
4.1 Deviance

The key building block of the deviance is a likelihood ratio comparison of the log-likelihood of current model against the log-likelihood of the best model which fits the observed data perfectly.
A perfectly fitting model is also called a saturated model because it uses as many estimated parameters (one for each observation) as there are observations.
A simple approach to obtain a saturated model is to substitute the observed dependent variable as predicted variable into the likelihood function, i.e., \(\hat{y}_i \leftarrow y_i\).
For example: In case of a binary distributed dependent variable \(y_i \in \{0,1\}\) with \(\hat{\pi}_i = y_i\) the likelihood function becomes

\[L(\text{saturated model}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} y_i^{y_i} \cdot (1-y_i)^{(1-y_i)} = 1\]

The deviance comparing the fitted model against a saturated model is simply a likelihood ratio statistic:

\[D(\text{fitted model}) = -2 \cdot \log\left(\frac{L(\text{fitted model})}{L(\text{saturated model})}\right) = -2 \cdot \log(L(\text{fitted model}))\]

Therefore, models with a small deviance fits the observed data well, whereas a model with a large deviance fits the data poorly.
Analog to linear regression one can think of the deviance as the residual-sum-of-squares.

title: Week08: Maximum Likelihood Principle (Abridged)
text:
4.2 Akaike Information Criterion

The Akaike Information Criterion (AIC) is a measure of model fit. It is defined as a deviance of a model which is penalized by its number of estimated parameters:

\[AIC = \underbrace{-2 \cdot \log(L(\text{model with } k \text{ parameters}))}_{= D(\text{model with } k \text{ parameters})} + 2 \cdot (k+1)\]

In general models with a smaller AIC are preferred.

title: Week08: Maximum Likelihood Principle (Abridged)
section: 5.1 Discussion the Logarithm and the Exponential Function (Skipped)
text:
5.1 Discussion the Logarithm and the Exponential Function (Skipped)

See the figure below for the exponential function and the logarithmic function


Exponential function: \(\exp(x)\) or \(e^x\) with \(e \approx 2.7183\).
Note: \(x \in [-\infty, \infty]\) but \(\exp(x) > 0\).
Natural logarithm: \(\ln(x)\), or \(\log_e(x)\).
It is the inverse of the exponential function.
Note: \(x > 0\) but \(\ln(x) \in [-\infty, \infty]\)

Meaning of inverse: \(x = \ln[\exp(x)]\) for \(x \in [-\infty, \infty]\) and \(x = \exp[\ln(x)]\) for \(x > 0\). These rules are often applied to linearize multiplicative functions.



Basic rules of exponential function:

\[\exp(-a) = \frac{1}{\exp(a)}\]
\[\exp(a+b) = \exp(a) \cdot \exp(b) \quad \text{and} \quad \exp(a-b) = \frac{\exp(a)}{\exp(b)}\]
\[\exp(b \cdot a) = [\exp(a)]^b \quad \text{or} \quad \exp(b \cdot a) = [\exp(b)]^a\]

Basic rules of logarithmic function:

\[\ln\left(\frac{1}{a}\right) = \ln(a^{-1}) = -\ln(a)\]
\[\ln(a \cdot b) = \ln(a) + \ln(b) \quad \text{and} \quad \ln\left(\frac{a}{b}\right) = \ln(a) - \ln(b)\]
\[\ln(a^b) = b \cdot \ln(a) \quad \text{and} \quad \ln(a^{-b}) = \ln\left(\frac{1}{a^b}\right) = -b \cdot \ln(a)\]

title: Week08: Maximum Likelihood Principle (Abridged)
text:
5.2 Discussion of Derivatives (skipped)

Derivatives of exp() and ln() (see slopes in graphs on page 1):

\[\frac{\partial \exp(x)}{\partial x} = \exp(x) \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{\partial \ln(x)}{\partial x} = \frac{1}{x}\]


General rule for derivatives of polynomial functions:

\[f(x) = a \cdot x^b \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\partial f(x)}{\partial x} = a \cdot b \cdot x^{b-1} \quad \text{Note: } x^0 = 1\]

Summation rule:

\[f(x) = g(x) + h(x) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\partial f(x)}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial g(x)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial h(x)}{\partial x}\]

Product rule:

\[f(x) = g(x) \cdot h(x) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\partial f(x)}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial g(x)}{\partial x} \cdot h(x) + g(x) \cdot \frac{\partial h(x)}{\partial x}\]
Example:
\[f(x) = x^2 \cdot \ln(x) \quad \text{with } g(x) = x^2 \text{ with } h(x) = \ln(x)\]
\[\frac{\partial f(x)}{\partial x} = 2x \cdot \ln(x) + x^2 \cdot \frac{1}{x} = x \cdot (2 \ln(x) + 1)\]


Chain rule of nested functions:

\[f(x) = g(h(x)) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\partial f(x)}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial g(h(x))}{\partial h(x)} \cdot \frac{\partial h(x)}{\partial x}\]
Example:
\[f(x) = \ln(3 + 2 \cdot x^2) \quad \text{with } g(h) = \ln(h) \text{ and } h(x) = 3 + 2 \cdot x^2\]
\[\frac{\partial f(x)}{\partial x} = \frac{1}{3 + 2 \cdot x^2} \cdot 4 \cdot x\]

Higher order derivatives:

\[\frac{\partial^2 f(x)}{\partial^2 x} = \frac{\partial \left(\frac{\partial f(x)}{\partial x}\right)}{\partial x}\]

Local minimum and maximum:
If \(\partial f(x) / \partial x = 0\) then \(f(x)\) has either a local minimum \(x_{min}\) or a local maximum \(x_{max}\).
Conditions for local minimum or maximum:

If \(\partial^2 f(x) / \partial^2 x < 0\) and \(\partial f(x) / \partial x = 0\) then \(f(x)\) has a local maximum \(x_{max}\).
If \(\partial^2 f(x) / \partial^2 x > 0\) and \(\partial f(x) / \partial x = 0\) then \(f(x)\) has a local minimum \(x_{min}\).




Example: Polynomial function of third degree (see the R script DerivativesCubicFct.R):

\[f(x) = 1 \cdot x^3 - 10 \cdot x^2 + 6 \cdot x + 20 \quad \text{(bold line for cubic function)}\]
\[\Rightarrow \frac{\partial f(x)}{\partial x} = 3 \cdot x^2 - 20 \cdot x + 6 \quad \text{(long-dashed line for quadratic function)}\]
\[\Rightarrow \frac{\partial^2 f(x)}{\partial^2 x} = 6 \cdot x - 20 \quad \text{(short-dashed line for linear function)}\]
![](images/w08_Maximum

Note: A quadratic function \(a \cdot x^2 + b \cdot x + c = 0\) potentially has two zero values at \(x_1 = \frac{-b - \sqrt{b^2 - 4 \cdot a \cdot c}}{2 \cdot a}\) and at \(x_2 = \frac{-b + \sqrt{b^2 - 4 \cdot a \cdot c}}{2 \cdot a}\).
Caution: If the discriminant \((b^2 - 4 \cdot a \cdot c)\) under the square root is negative then the solution will consist of complex conjugates (pairs of imaginary numbers).
The first derivative is a quadratic function. Therefore, \(3 \cdot x^2 - 20 \cdot x + 6 = 0\) has local maximum or minimum either at

\[x_1 = \frac{20 - \sqrt{(-20)^2 - 4 \cdot (3 \cdot 6)}}{3 \cdot 2} = \frac{10}{3} - \frac{\sqrt{82}}{3}\]
\[x_2 = \frac{20 + \sqrt{(-20)^2 - 4 \cdot (3 \cdot 6)}}{3 \cdot 2} = \frac{10}{3} + \frac{\sqrt{82}}{3}\]
The second derivative is \(\frac{\partial^2 f(x)}{\partial^2 x} = 6 \cdot x - 20\).
For \(x_1\) we get \(6 \cdot \left[\frac{10}{3} - \frac{\sqrt{82}}{3}\right] - 20 = -2 \cdot \sqrt{82} < 0\) which therefore is a local maximum
and for \(x_2\) we get \(6 \cdot \left[\frac{10}{3} + \frac{\sqrt{82}}{3}\right] - 20 = 2 \cdot \sqrt{82} > 0\) which is a local minimum.

title: Week07: Poor Man’s Fourier Analysis: Analysis of Periodic Data with Ordinary Least Squares
text:
1 Modeling Periodic Variations in Sequential Data1

We can model oscillating or circular data \(y(t)\), assuming a cycle length of \(\eta\) observations, by a periodic function

\[y(t) = \beta_0 + a \cdot \cos(2 \cdot \pi \cdot \lambda \cdot (t - \varphi)) + \varepsilon(t) \quad \text{where}\] (1.1)

\(t\) is the index of the individual data points in an equally spaced sequence of data,
\(a\) is the amplitude of the oscillation,
\(\lambda = 1/\eta\) is the frequency of the oscillation, and
\(\varphi\) is the phase shift in observations with \(\varphi \in [0, \eta - 1]\).

The phase shift is that index in the sequence of observations relative to the starting observation where the oscillation peaks for the first time, that is, the cosine function is at its maximum.

Example: for monthly temperature data with a cycle length of \(\eta = 12\) of equally spaced data points the frequency is \(\lambda = \frac{1}{12}\). The amplitude measures how high and low the temperatures swing around the average annual temperature measured by \(\beta_0\). Assuming the sequence starts at January, which is indexed by \(t = 0\), for summer temperatures on the northern hemisphere the phase shift will be \(\varphi \approx 7\), that is, the temperature peaks in August (the 8th month of the year).
This non-linear periodic specification in equation (1.1) with an unknown phase shift \(\varphi\) and an amplitude \(a\) cannot be directly estimated with OLS.
However, it has an equivalent linear model specification in the unknown parameters \(\beta_{cos}\) and \(\beta_{sin}\) given by

\[y(t) = \beta_0 + \beta_{cos} \cdot \cos(2 \cdot \pi \cdot \lambda \cdot t) + \beta_{sin} \cdot \sin(2 \cdot \pi \cdot \lambda \cdot t) + \varepsilon(t)\] (1.2)
The two proxy variables \(\cos(2 \cdot \pi \cdot \lambda \cdot t)\) and \(\sin(2 \cdot \pi \cdot \lambda \cdot t)\) only depend on the index \(t\) and the hypothetical frequency \(\lambda\). These proxy variables can be calculated for any given equally spaced sequence of data by using the sequence index \(t\) and a frequency \(\lambda\).

After some algebra the amplitude can be expressed by

\[a = \sqrt{\beta_{cos}^2 + \beta_{sin}^2}\]
and the phase shift by
\[\varphi = \arctan(\beta_{sin} / \beta_{cos})\]
Important: see the discussion of cases below.

In most applications only the amplitude at a given frequency \(\lambda\) is of interest because its magnitude expresses the contribution of the particular oscillation to the series.
Note that a flat line has an amplitude of zero and thus does not contribute anything in explaining the variation of \(y(t)\).
Because the amplitude and phase shift are calculated non-linearly from the two estimated OLS parameters \(\beta_{cos}\) and \(\beta_{sin}\), their standard errors are not directly known and must be approximated by using the delta-method. See R-function car::deltaMethod().
The R-script SINCOSREGRESSION.R demonstrates this modeling approach with a simulated series. The amplitude and the phase shift as well as their standard errors are calculated in this script by the function ampPhase().


1 In contrast to a full-fledged Fourier analysis, which evaluates the contribution of oscillations with a given frequencies to the periodic variability of a sequence of data over a the whole spectrum of frequencies, the proposed “poor man’s Fourier analysis” here only performs one evaluation at a given frequency. The contribution of an oscillation is measured in terms of its amplitude.


2 Review: Some Basic Trigonometric Rules
The following trigonometric definitions and relationships are frequently used:

The \(\sin(\phi)\) and \(\cos(\phi)\) functions in dependence of an angle \(\phi\) are graphed in Figure 1a for a standard circle with a radius of 1. Note: due to the Pythagoras theorem both functions satisfy the constraint \(\sin^2(\phi) + \cos^2(\phi) = 1\). Furthermore, the sin- and cos-functions are uncorrelated \(\int_0^{2\pi} \sin(\phi) \cdot \cos(\phi) \cdot d\phi = 0\).
This implies that both proxy variables \(\cos(2 \cdot \pi \cdot \lambda \cdot t)\) and \(\sin(2 \cdot \pi \cdot \lambda \cdot t)\) are also uncorrelated.
The tangents function is defined by \(\tan(\phi) \equiv \sin(\phi)/\cos(\phi)\) and is graphed in Figure 1b.
The relationship between angles in degrees and angles in radians is shown in Figure 1a. All angles \(\phi\) in this discussion are assumed to be in radians with \(\phi_{radian} \in [0, 2 \cdot \pi]\). The transformation between both angular measurement units is \(\phi_{degree} = \phi_{radian}/(2 \cdot \pi) \cdot 360\) with \(\pi \approx 3.1415\).




Figure 1: The trigonometric sin, cos and tan functions



To retrieve an angle \(\phi\) from the point coordinates \((\cos(\phi), \sin(\phi))\) on the circle, one can use the inverse tangents function2 \(\phi = \arctan(\sin(\phi)/\cos(\phi))\).
However the tangents is not a bijective function, that is, it lacks a unique one-to-one relationship, and it has poles at \(\frac{\pi}{2}\) and \(\frac{3\pi}{2}\) (see Figure 1b), one needs to distinguish between three different cases for the ratio \(x = \sin(\phi)/\cos(\phi)\)

\[\phi = \begin{cases}
\arctan(x) & \text{if } \cos(\phi) > 0 \text{ and } \sin(\phi) > 0 \\
\pi + \arctan(x) & \text{if } \cos(\phi) < 0 \text{ with either } \sin(\phi) < 0 \text{ or } \sin(\phi) > 0 \\
2 \cdot \pi + \arctan(x) & \text{if } \cos(\phi) > 0 \text{ and } \sin(\phi) < 0
\end{cases}\]
to obtain the desired angle \(\phi\), which is equivalent to the phase shift.

2 One needs to work carefully here, R provides two different arc-tangents functions. The function atan2() provides direct results but cannot be used with the car::deltaMethod() function. The function atan() requires the user to first distinguish between the three different cases. The function atan() work well with car::deltaMethod().

title: Week06: Appendix03 - Principal Components Overview
text:
For a dataset with \(N\) observations and \(P\) features. \(P\) is a column perspective and \(N\) is the row perspective of the individual observations.


[a] Feature selection: Reduce the dimensionality for a model calibration by setting irrelevant parameters to zero, i.e., they don’t influence the model anymore. Example: Irrelevant features are suppressed by setting their parameters to zero (or close to zero as in Ridge/Lasso regression).
The reduction (or down-weighting) of features will reduce the variance at the possible expense of raising the bias.
[b] Principal components: Account for redundancy among relevant features. Example: Two perfectly correlated features in essence contain the same information, thus one feature is sufficient to capture exhaustively the signal of both features on the target variable. For less than perfectly correlated features we combine the shared information in both features into one underlying latent feature (see definition of latent variables). The remainder unique information in both features is uncorrelated with the newly generated latent feature.





Principal component analysis in an unsupervised method because it solely uses information in the features by ignoring the target variable.
All features are \(z\)-transformed.
The \(m^{th}\) principal component \(Z_m\) is defined as a linear combination of the \(P\) features: \(Z_m = \sum_{j=1}^{P} \phi_{jm} \cdot X_j\). The determination of the loadings \(\phi_{jm}\) is achieved with the method of principal components, which is based on a eigenvalue-eigenvector decomposition.
High component loadings with \(|\phi_{km}|\) close to one group highly correlated features, either positive or negative, together into the \(m^{th}\) component. Whereas a loading close to zero excludes feature from the \(m^{th}\) principal component \(Z_m\).
The key to principal components selection is that each component summarizes maximum information and independent information within its constituting variables.
The first component captures most of the variability in the features, second component comprises the largest variation of the remaining variation being uncorrelated with the previous component and so forth.
The components are uncorrelated with each other, because \(\sum_{k=1}^{P} \phi_{km} \cdot \phi_{kl} = 0\) and \(\sum_{k=1}^{P} \phi_{km}^2 = 1\).
Special case: Theoretically, there are as many components as there are features, however, many components are irrelevant. For \(M = P\) the estimated coefficients regression coefficients \(\beta_k\) of an constituting variable \(k\) are a function of the principal component weights \(\phi_{km}\) and the component regression coefficient \(\theta_m\):

\[\beta_k = \sum_{m=1}^{M} \theta_m \cdot \phi_{km}\]
Thus, for \(M = P\) no information is lost.




PLS is a supervised method because it is calculated with target variable \(\mathbf{y}\) in mind.
Usually, the independent features \(\mathbf{X}\) are \(z\)-transformed.
Algorithm:

Calculate the coefficients \(\phi_{k1}\) as the bivariate regression coefficients between the feature \(X_k\) and the target variable \(Y\). The bivariate regression coefficient is proportional to the correlation coefficient.
The first PLS component becomes \(Z_1 = \sum_{j=1}^{P} \phi_{j1} \cdot X_j\).
Calculate the residuals \(e_{j1}\) for each feature by regressing the \(X_j\) on \(Z_1\), i.e., the remainder of \(X_j\) not being correlated with \(Z_1\). Thus, they hold no information of \(Z_1\) and any of its underlying variables \(X_j\).
Use the coefficients \(\phi_{j2}\) of a bivariate regression of the residuals \(e_{j1}\) on the target variable \(Y\).
Calculate the second PLS component by \(Z_2 = \sum_{j=1}^{P} \phi_{j2} \cdot e_{j1}\).
Repeat steps 3, 4, and 5 by calculating at the \(p\)-th step the residuals \(e_{j,p-1}\) by regressing the \(e_{j,p-2}\) on \(Z_{p-1}\). Then obtain \(\phi_{jp}\) by regressing \(Y\) on \(e_{jp}\) and calculate \(Z_p = \sum_{j=1}^{P} \phi_{pj} \cdot e_{j,p-1}\).

Since the PLS components \(Z_p\) are based on residual calculations, the PLS components are uncorrelated with the PLS components already in the model





Figure 4.8: A diagram depicting the differences between PCR (left) and PLS (right). PCR finds principal components (PCs) that maximally summarize the features independent of the response variable and then uses those PCs as predictor variables. PLS finds components that simultaneously summarize variation of the predictors while being optimally correlated with the outcome and then uses those PCs as predictors.
