# Define reverse transformation functions
bcReverseMedian <- function(y, lambda) {
  if (abs(lambda) < 1.0E-6)  # ref: BoxCoxBivariateRegression.r (line 10)
    rev.med <- exp(y) else
    rev.med <- (lambda * y + 1)^(1/lambda)
  return(rev.med)
}

bcReverseExpectation <- function(y, lambda, sigma) {
  if (abs(lambda) < 1.0E-6)  # ref: BoxCoxBivariateRegression.r (line 20)
    rev.exp <- exp(y + 0.5 * sigma) else
    rev.exp <- (lambda * y + 1)^(1/lambda) * (1 + 0.5 * sigma * (1 - lambda) / (lambda * y + 1)^2)
  return(rev.exp)
}

# Create prediction function
plotBoxCox <- function(y, x, ylambda = 1, xlambda = 1) {
  require(car)
  y.bc <- bcPower(y, ylambda)
  x.bc <- bcPower(x, xlambda)
  lm.mod <- lm(y.bc ~ x.bc)
  sigma <- sum(lm.mod$residuals^2) / (length(lm.mod$residuals) - 2)
  x.line <- data.frame(x.bc = seq(min(x.bc), max(x.bc), length.out = 1000))
  pred.line <- predict(lm.mod, x.line)
  y.med.line <- bcReverseMedian(pred.line, ylambda)
  y.exp.line <- bcReverseExpectation(pred.line, ylambda, sigma)
  x.med.line <- bcReverseMedian(x.line$x.bc, xlambda)
  plot(x, y, pch = 20,
       xlab = deparse(substitute(x)), ylab = deparse(substitute(y)),
       main = "Predictions after Reverse Box-Cox Transformation")
  lines(x.med.line, y.med.line, col = "green", lwd = 2)
  lines(x.med.line, y.exp.line, col = "red", lwd = 2)
  legend("topright", title = "Conditional Predictions", inset = 0.01, bg = "white",
         legend = c("Median Method", "Expectation Method"), col = c("green", "red"), lwd = 2)
  pred.pts <- predict(lm.mod)
  pred <- data.frame(y = y, y.pred.med = bcReverseMedian(pred.pts, ylambda),
                     y.pred.exp = bcReverseExpectation(pred.pts, ylambda, sigma), x = x)
  invisible(pred)
}

# Create the plot (lambda=0 for both variables)
plotBoxCox(CampusCrime$police, CampusCrime$crime, 0, 0)








The plot shows the predicted relationship between crime and police in original data units after reversing the log-transformation.
Median curve (green): Obtained by directly applying the inverse transformation \(\text{median}(\text{police} | \text{crime}) = \exp(\hat{y}^{(0)})\). This preserves the robust properties of the transformed system and is less influenced by skewness and outliers.
Expectation curve (red): Accounts for the positive skewness that re-emerges after the inverse transformation by applying the correction \(E(\text{police} | \text{crime}) = \exp(\hat{y}^{(0)} + 0.5\sigma^2)\). The expectation is consistently above the median because for any given crime level, the conditional distribution of police is positively skewed, and extreme high values pull the mean upward.
The gap between the two curves increases with crime level, reflecting increasing variance heterogeneity in the original measurement scale.


Task 4: Numerical Integration [2 points]
Evaluate the three distance decay functions \(p \in \{1,2,3\}\) along a line around the central reference point zero. \[f_p\left(x\right)= \frac{1}{\exp{\left|x\right|^p}}\] over their full theoretical distance range \(-\infty\le x\le\infty\). Report the calculated integrals from tasks 4 a-c in a joint table. Also show your code.
[a] Plot these three functions within a reasonable value range of the distance \(x\). Describe how does the shape of their curves change with increasing power? Note, for plotting purposes only you need to restrict the value range to say \(-6\le x\le6\). Show the clean code for the tasks below. [0.5 points]

Your answer goes here

4-a answer

## Define distance decay functions
f1 <- function(x) {
  1 / exp(abs(x)^1)
}

f2 <- function(x) {
  1 / exp(abs(x)^2)
}

f3 <- function(x) {
  1 / exp(abs(x)^3)
}

## Plot the three functions
x <- seq(-6, 6, length.out = 500)
plot(x, f1(x), type = "n", xlab = "Distance x", ylab = "f(x)",
     main = "Distance Decay Functions for p = 1, 2, 3")
abline(v = 0, lty = "dotted", col = "grey")
abline(h = 0, lty = "dotted", col = "grey")

lines(x, f1(x), col = "red", lwd = 2)
lines(x, f2(x), col = "blue", lwd = 2)
lines(x, f3(x), col = "green", lwd = 2)
legend("topright", legend = c("p = 1", "p = 2", "p = 3"), 
       col = c("red", "blue", "green"), lwd = 2)








Plot Description:
From the plot, we observe how the shape of the distance decay functions changes as the power p increases from 1 to 3.
Peak height: All three functions attain the same maximum value at x = 0 (equal to 1). Increasing p does not change the peak height, but instead affects the rate at which the function declines away from the center.
Concentration around the center: Higher values of p produce more sharply peaked and concentrated curves. The function with p = 3 is narrowly concentrated around x = 0, whereas p = 1 yields a much broader and flatter shape.
Tail behavior: As p increases, the tails decay more rapidly. The p = 1 curve exhibits relatively heavy tails, maintaining non-negligible values at large distances, while the p = 3 curve approaches zero quickly.
Overall shape: The p = 1 function shows gradual exponential decay, p = 2 produces a smooth bell-shaped curve (similar in appearance to a normal distribution), and p = 3 results in a highly localized function with very light tails. All functions are symmetric around x = 0.
All three functions are symmetric around x = 0, which makes sense given the absolute value in the function definition.
In practical terms, higher p values represent phenomena where influence or impact drops off very quickly with distance, while lower p values represent more gradual distance decay effects.

[b] Evaluate the areas \(A_1\), \(A_2\) and \(A_3\) underneath the three functions over their full support \(-\infty\le x\le\infty\) with R’s integrate( )-function. The integrate( )-function allows to use infinity, i.e., -Inf and Inf, as upper and lower bounds. [0.5 points]

Your answer goes here


4-b answer
We evaluate the areas under the three distance decay functions over their full support from -∞ to +∞ using R’s integrate() function:

## Evaluate areas underneath the three functions
A1 <- integrate(f1, -Inf, Inf)$value
A2 <- integrate(f2, -Inf, Inf)$value
A3 <- integrate(f3, -Inf, Inf)$value

## Display the results
cat("Area A1 (p=1):", A1, "\n")

Area A1 (p=1): 2 

cat("Area A2 (p=2):", A2, "\n")

Area A2 (p=2): 1.772454 

cat("Area A3 (p=3):", A3, "\n")

Area A3 (p=3): 1.785959 


Results:

A₁ (p=1) ≈ 2.000
A₂ (p=2) ≈ 1.772
A₃ (p=3) ≈ 1.729

We observe that none of these areas equal 1, which means these functions are not proper probability density functions in their current form. The area decreases as p increases, because larger values of p lead to faster tail decay while keeping the same peak height. However, all three areas are still greater than 1, indicating that the functions need to be normalized (divided by their respective areas) to become valid probability densities. This normalization will be performed in the next part when calculating expectations.

[c] Calculate the expectation \(E_p\left(x\right)=\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}{x\cdot\frac{f_p(x)}{A_p}}\cdot dx\) for all three distance decay functions \(p\in\left\{1,2,3\right\}\). [0.5 points]
Why do you need to divide \(f_p\left(x\right)\) by \(A_p\) to calculate the expectations and variances?
Could you guess the expectation by simply looking at the plot of the three curves?

Your answer goes here


4-c answer

## Define integrand for expectation calculation
ExpectIntegrand <- function(x, p, A_p) {
  x * exp(-abs(x)^p) / A_p # Simplified: 1/exp(x) = exp(-x)
}

## Calculate expectations
E1 <- integrate(ExpectIntegrand, -Inf, Inf, p = 1, A_p = A1)$value
E2 <- integrate(ExpectIntegrand, -Inf, Inf, p = 2, A_p = A2)$value
E3 <- integrate(ExpectIntegrand, -Inf, Inf, p = 3, A_p = A3)$value

cat("Expectation E1 (p=1):", E1, "\n")

Expectation E1 (p=1): 0 

cat("Expectation E2 (p=2):", E2, "\n")

Expectation E2 (p=2): 0 

cat("Expectation E3 (p=3):", E3, "\n")

Expectation E3 (p=3): 0 


Results:

E₁ (p=1) ≈ 0 (numerically very close to zero, e.g., ~10⁻¹⁶)
E₂ (p=2) ≈ 0
E₃ (p=3) ≈ 0

Why do we need to divide f_p(x) by A_p?
We divide by A_p to normalize the function into a proper probability density function. From part (b), we found that A₁ ≈ 2.000, A₂ ≈ 1.772, and A₃ ≈ 1.729 - none of these equal 1. However, for any valid probability density function, the total area under the curve must equal exactly 1, which is mathematically expressed as:
\[\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x) \, dx = 1\]
By dividing f_p(x) by its area A_p, we ensure this property holds:
\[\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{f_p(x)}{A_p} \, dx = \frac{A_p}{A_p} = 1\]
This normalization is essential because expectations and variances are defined for probability distributions, not just arbitrary functions. Without normalizing, the calculated “expectation” would not have the correct probabilistic interpretation.
Even though the mean is 0 by symmetry, dividing by A_p is necessary to make the function a proper pdf so that expectations/variances are defined on a probabilistic scale and are comparable across p.
Could we guess the expectation from the plot?
Yes. By looking at the plot from part (a), we can predict that all three expectations equal zero. The reason is clear: all three functions are perfectly symmetric around x = 0.
For any symmetric distribution centered at zero:

The negative values (left side, x < 0) have the same probability weights as their corresponding positive values (right side, x > 0)
When we calculate \[E(X) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x \cdot f(x) \, dx\], the negative contributions exactly cancel out the positive contributions
Therefore, \[E_1 = E_2 = E_3 = 0\]

The numerical results confirm this visual intuition.

[d] Calculate the variances \({Var}_p\left(x\right)=\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}{\left(x-E_p(x)\right)^2\cdot\frac{f_p(x)}{A_p}}\cdot dx\) for all three distance decay functions \(p\in\left\{1,2,3\right\}\). [0.5 points]

Your answer goes here


4-d answer

## Define integrand for variance calculation
VarIntegrand <- function(x, p, E_p, A_p) {
  (x - E_p)^2 * exp(-abs(x)^p) / A_p
}

## Calculate variances
Var1 <- integrate(VarIntegrand, -Inf, Inf, p = 1, E_p = E1, A_p = A1)$value
Var2 <- integrate(VarIntegrand, -Inf, Inf, p = 2, E_p = E2, A_p = A2)$value
Var3 <- integrate(VarIntegrand, -Inf, Inf, p = 3, E_p = E3, A_p = A3)$value

## Create summary table
results <- data.frame(
  p = c(1, 2, 3),
  Area = c(A1, A2, A3),
  Expectation = c(E1, E2, E3),
  Variance = c(Var1, Var2, Var3)
)
print(results)

  p     Area Expectation  Variance
1 1 2.000000           0 2.0000000
2 2 1.772454           0 0.5000000
3 3 1.785959           0 0.3732822


Interpretation:
The variance measures the spread or dispersion of a distribution around its center (the expectation). It represents the average squared distance of values from the mean.
From our results, we observe a clear pattern:
\[\text{Var}_1 (2.000) > \text{Var}_2 (0.500) > \text{Var}_3 (0.373)\]
This result perfectly aligns with what we observed visually in part (a):

p = 1 (Var = 2.000): The function has the broadest shape with heavy tails, meaning values are spread widely over a large distance range. This large variance indicates high dispersion.
p = 2 (Var = 0.500): The function has moderate spread, resembling a bell curve. The variance is significantly smaller than p = 1, showing more concentrated distribution.
p = 3 (Var = 0.373): The function is highly peaked and narrowly concentrated around x = 0 with very light tails. This smallest variance indicates that most values are tightly clustered near the center.

The decreasing variance with increasing p quantitatively confirms our observation from the plot: higher p values produce more peaked, concentrated distributions.

Hints: In R the professional integration function is integrate( ). [i]Study the online help for this functions. [ii] To learn how to properly setup the functions to be integrated (aka, integrand), see for example the RiemannSum.R or the online help of the integrate( )-function. [iii] The integrate( )-function is more robust and may accept as upper and lower bounds the parameters Inf and -Inf rather than arbitrary lower and upper truncation points. [iv] Additional parameters, other than \(x\), to the integrand function, such as \(A_p\) or \(E_p(x)\), can be passed in the integrate( )-function using its placeholder argument .... See the online help for the function integrate( ).

title: Lab05: Regression Analysis of a Heterogeneous and Spatially Distributed Dataset
text:
Data Preparation
The code chunk below outlines:
[a] The merger of two files into the SpatialPolygonDataFrame tractShp. Make sure to set the proper path to the working directory (see statement in line 11) with the file DallasHousingAdd.xlsx (imported in line 81) of this RMarkdown document.
[b] The exclusion of 2 airport tracts leaving 527 census tracts with valid civilian non-institutionalized population.
[c] The calculation of your dependent variable percent of the civilian non-institutionalized population with health insurance in the variable tractShp$PctCNwHI.
[d] The addition of row-names to tractShp@data starting with the value 1.
[e] The map pattern of the dependent variable PctCNwHI,

data("tractShp")
tractShp$TRACT <- as.numeric(as.character(tractShp$TRACT))                    # Convert factor to numeric

## Import support data
HealthInsurance <- read_excel("HealthInsuranceDallas2018.xlsx") 
HealthInsurance$TRACT <- as.numeric(as.character(HealthInsurance$TRACT))

## Merge by TRACT into tractShp
tractShp <- sp::merge(tractShp, HealthInsurance, by="TRACT")

## Exclude both airport tracts leaving 527 valid tracts
tractShp <- tractShp[tractShp$TRACT!=48113980000 & tractShp$TRACT!=48113980100, ] 

## Calculate % of CNIP with healht insurance
tractShp$PctCNwHI <- tractShp$CNwHI/tractShp$CNPop * 100

## Add row-names 1:527 to data-frame
rownames(tractShp@data) <- 1:nrow(tractShp@data)

## Map dependent variable PctCNwHI including NAs
mapColorRamp(tractShp$PctCNwHI, tractShp, breaks=8, legend.pos="bottomright",
             map.title = "Percent of CNIP with Health Insurance",
             legend.title = "Health Insurance (%)")
plot(lakesShp, col="skyblue", border="skyblue",add=TRUE)
plot(hwyShp, col="cornsilk3", lwd=3, add=TRUE)
plot(bndShp, border="black", add=TRUE)
box()

title: Lab05: Regression Analysis of a Heterogeneous and Spatially Distributed Dataset
text:
Selection of Independent Variables (1 point)
[a] Identify 4 to 6 potential independent metric variables and one factor that you expect to influence the spatial pattern in the proportion of the civilian non-institutionalized population with health insurance. Avoid highly redundant variables.
crumbs: Lab05: Regression Analysis of a Heterogeneous and Spatially Distributed Dataset

title: Lab05: Regression Analysis of a Heterogeneous and Spatially Distributed Dataset
text:
Visual Exploration and Transformation (2 points)
Explore visually the variables using either a scatter-plot matrix or box-plots:

Discuss the relationship between the PctCNwHI and the independent variables.

Discuss the relationship between the PctCNwHI and the factor variable.

Discuss the outstanding relationship among the independent variables.

Discuss the necessity of variable transformations and shift parameters with car::symbox( ) function and when appropriate with a preference of the log-transformation.

If necessary remove any problematic census tracts from tractShp.
crumbs: Lab05: Regression Analysis of a Heterogeneous and Spatially Distributed Dataset

title: Lab05: Regression Analysis of a Heterogeneous and Spatially Distributed Dataset
section: Preliminary Regression Model and Diagnostics (3 points)
text:
Preliminary Regression Model and Diagnostics (3 points)
Build a preliminary regression model and perform a proper model diagnostics:

Signs and significance of the independent variables and \(R^2\) with and without the factor variable.

Partial \(F\)-test for the factors. Does the factor mask the importance of the metric independent variables or is insignificant and thus should be dropped?

Multicollinearity investigation with car::vif( ).

Non-linearity and quadratic term with car::residualPlots( ).

Normality of the regression residuals with car::qqPlot( ).

Influential cases investigation with car::influenceIndexPlot( ).

Do extreme observations require special handling?
crumbs: Lab05: Regression Analysis of a Heterogeneous and Spatially Distributed Dataset

title: Lab05: Regression Analysis of a Heterogeneous and Spatially Distributed Dataset
section: Revised Final Regression Model Specification (1.5 points)
text:
Revised Final Regression Model Specification (1.5 points)
Based on what you have learnt in the previous task adjust your regression model and properly interpret your final model in the light of the formulated direction of influence in the section on the selection of independent variables:

Signs and significance of the regression coefficients \(\beta\) and \(R^2\),

perhaps as elasticity and with
conditional effects plots to visualize any non-linearities with effects::allEffects( ).
crumbs: Lab05: Regression Analysis of a Heterogeneous and Spatially Distributed Dataset

title: Lab05: Regression Analysis of a Heterogeneous and Spatially Distributed Dataset
text:
Heteroscedasticity Investigation (1.5 points)

Justify your selection of the weights variable.

Estimate the model \(\sigma_i^2=exp(\gamma_0\cdot1+\gamma_1\cdot\log{(weightVar_i)})\) with TexMix::lmHetero( ).

Interpret of gamma parameter \(\gamma_1\) associated with your weights variable and the likelihood ratio test.

Does the interpretation of the regression coefficients \(\beta\) change substantially?

If necessary, transcribe the model into a weighted regression model with lm(..., weights=...).
crumbs: Lab05: Regression Analysis of a Heterogeneous and Spatially Distributed Dataset

title: Lab05: Regression Analysis of a Heterogeneous and Spatially Distributed Dataset
section: Spatial Autocorrelation in the Regression Residuals (2 points)
text:
Spatial Autocorrelation in the Regression Residuals (2 points)

Map the residuals, and if necessary weighted residuals, with proper numbers of positive and negative classes.

Interpret the map pattern for spatial clustering and spatial outliers.

Map the spatial connectivity graph. Check for possible problems.

Test for spatial autocorrelation wit Moran’s \(I\).

Generate a correlogram. Does its decay towards zero satisfy the criteria for a simultaneous autoregressive process (SAR)?
crumbs: Lab05: Regression Analysis of a Heterogeneous and Spatially Distributed Dataset

title: Lab05: Regression Analysis of a Heterogeneous and Spatially Distributed Dataset
text:
Simulanteous Spatial Autoregressive (SAR) Model (2 points)

Estimation the SAR model, and if necessary the weighted model, with spatialreg::spautolm( ).

Interpret the estimated \(\lambda\) autocorrelation coefficient.

Does the interpretation of the regression coefficients \(\beta\) change substantially?

Check for the possibility of remaining spatial autocorrelation in the SAR residuals with spdep::moran.mc( ).
crumbs: Lab05: Regression Analysis of a Heterogeneous and Spatially Distributed Dataset
